message.content:
Adversarially VERIFY this research claim about how a top deep-research system scores on DeepResearch Bench.

CLAIM: What actually separates the #1 system from Tavily is CROSS-SOURCE RECONCILIATION IN PROSE. On task 72 cellcog-max writes 12,937 words (2.15x Tavily), 32 H3, 168 prose paragraphs averaging 75 words, and ZERO bullets, with 2.40 contrast/inference markers per 1k words vs Tavily's 0.83 (and Tavily's corpus-wide 0.43/1k is the LOWEST of the 11 systems I measured).
DETAIL: Tavily's prose under a subsection reads: bolded topic sentence, then 'Evidence from advanced economies:' then bullets, each bullet = one source's finding with a citation marker. It LISTS. cellcog-max's section 8.2 'Outstanding Tensions and Unresolved Questions' reads verbatim: 'The most fundamental is the gap between task-level productivity evidence and aggregate employment outcomes. The micro-level experiments consistently show large productivity gains; the macro-level evidence shows modest or null employment effects. This could mean that AI's labor market impact is still early and will intensify; that productivity gains are being absorbed through shorter hours, task expansion, or organizational slack rather than headcount reduction; or that general equilibrium effects ... are already offsetting displacement. The current evidence cannot definitively distinguish among these explanations.' That is a single paragraph that (a) names a contradiction BETWEEN two literatures, (b) enumerates three candidate reconciliations, (c) states what the evidence cannot settle. No sentence in it is span-groundable to one source -- which is exactly the sentence type POLARIS's per-sentence span-grounding constraint structurally forbids. Also note cellcog's H3 titles are CLAIM-BEARING ('The Skill Compression Finding', 'The Jagged Frontier: Limits of Augmentation', 'The Denmark-U.S. Divergence: Institutions as the Key Variable', 'The Entry-Level Bottleneck') while Tavily's are bare TOPIC LABELS ('Gender', 'Age', 'Healthcare'). Cheap, high-leverage change: make subsection headings assert a finding rather than name a topic.
SYSTEM: other
SOURCE GIVEN: https://huggingface.co/spaces/muset-ai/DeepResearch-Bench-Leaderboard/resolve/main/data/raw_data/drb_cellcog_max.jsonl
STATED CONFIDENCE: high

Go to the source. Check it actually says this. Check it is CURRENT (the benchmark's leaderboard and evaluator both
changed in 2026 — the original paper's numbers are stale; Gemini-2.5-Pro was replaced by GPT-5.5 as the RACE judge).
Default to UNSUPPORTED if you cannot confirm it from a primary source. We would rather have five verified facts
than thirty plausible ones.

attachment.content:
- deep-research: Deep research harness — fan-out web searches, fetch sources, adversarially verify claims, synthesize a cited report. - When the user wants a deep, multi-source, fact-checked research report on any topic. BEFORE invoking, check if the question is specific enough to research directly — if underspecified (e.g., "what car to buy" without budget/use-case/region), ask 2-3 clarifying questions to narrow scope. Then pass the refined question as args, weaving the answers in.
- dataviz: Use this skill whenever you are about to create ANY chart, graph, plot, dashboard, or data visualization, in ANY output medium — an HTML or React artifact, inline SVG, plotting code in any library (matplotlib, plotly, d3, Recharts, …), an image/PNG you will render and upload, or a chart shared into Slack. Read it BEFORE writing the first line of chart code, choosing chart colors, building a stat tile / meter / KPI row, or laying out a dashboard. Produces visualizations that read as one system — elegant, accessible, consistent in light and dark — using a brand-neutral placeholder palette you swap for your own. Teaches a design-system-agnostic method: a form heuristic, a color formula with a runnable validator, mark specs, and interaction rules. A validated default palette is documented in `references/palette.md` — swap that file's values for your brand's. Triggers on: "chart", "graph", "plot", "data viz", "visualization", "dashboard", "analytics", "visualize data", "categorical colors", "sequential / diverging palette", "stat tile", "sparkline", "heatmap", "legend", "axis", "tooltip", "chart colors", "color by series".
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by exercising it end-to-end and observing behavior — drive the affected flow, not just tests or typecheck. Run before committing nontrivial changes; bootstraps this repo's project verify skill if none exists yet. Don't invoke it on a diff that only touches tests, docs, or other code with no runtime surface to drive (a change to product source always has one) — there's nothing to observe.
- code-review: Review the current diff for correctness bugs and reuse/simplification/efficiency cleanups at the given effort level (low/medium: fewer, high-confidence findings; high→max: broader coverage, may include uncertain findings; ultra: deep multi-agent review in the cloud). Pass --comment to post findings as inline PR comments, or --fix to apply the findings to the working tree after the review.
- simplify: Review the changed code for reuse, simplification, efficiency, and altitude cleanups, then apply the fixes. Quality only — it does not hunt for bugs; use /code-review for that.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled cloud agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring cloud agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Reference for the Claude API / Anthropic SDK — model ids, pricing, params, streaming, tool use, MCP, agents, caching, token counting, model migration.
TRIGGER — read BEFORE opening the target file; don't skip because it "looks like a one-liner" — whenever: the prompt names Claude/Anthropic in any form (Claude, Anthropic, Fable, Opus, Sonnet, Haiku, `anthropic`, `@anthropic-ai`, `claude-*`, `us.anthropic.*`, `[1m]`); the user asks about an LLM (pricing/model choice/limits/caching) — never answer from memory; OR the task is LLM-shaped with provider unstated (agent/MCP/tool-definition/multi-agent/RAG/LLM-judge/computer-use; generate/summarize/extract/classify/rewrite/converse over NL; debugging refusals/cutoffs/streaming/tool-calls/tokens).
SKIP only when another provider is being worked on (overrides all triggers): OpenAI/GPT/Gemini/Llama/Mistral/Cohere/Ollama named in the query; OR `grep -rE 'openai|langchain_openai|google.generativeai|genai|mistralai|cohere|ollama'` over the project hits (run this grep FIRST if no provider named — don't Read the file).
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a GitHub pull request; for your working diff use /code-review
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

input.command:
python3 - <<'EOF'
import json,re
def load(f):
    return {r['id']:r for r in (json.loads(l) for l in open('drb_corpus/%s.jsonl'%f))}
c=load('cellcog'); t=load('tavily')
print(c[72]['prompt'][:300])
print('---')
for name,d in [('cellcog',c),('tavily',t)]:
    a=d[72]['article']
    words=len(a.split())
    h3=len(re.findall(r'^### ',a,re.M))
    h2=len(re.findall(r'^## ',a,re.M))
    bullets=len(re.findall(r'^\s*[-*+] ',a,re.M))
    paras=[p for p in a.split('\n\n') if p.strip() and not p.strip().startswith('#') and not re.match(r'^\s*[-*+|>\d]',p.strip())]
    print(name, 'words',words,'h2',h2,'h3',h3,'bullets',bullets,'paras',len(paras),'avg para words', round(sum(len(p.split()) for p in paras)/max(len(paras),1),1))
EOF

content.content:
"structures, training institutions, and the broader policy environment.

### 8.2 Outstanding Tensions and Unresolved Questions

Several important tensions in the literature remain unresolved. The most fundamental is the gap between task-level productivity evidence and aggregate employment outcomes. The micro-level experiments consistently show large productivity gains; the macro-level evidence shows modest or null employment effects. This could mean that AI's labor market impact is still early and will intensify; that productivity gains are being absorbed through shorter hours, task expansion, or organizational slack rather than headcount reduction; or that general equilibrium effects — new demand, new products, new tasks — are already offsetting displacement. The current evidence cannot definitively distinguish among these explanations.

A second tension concerns the temporal horizon. Most of the evidence reviewed here covers at most two to three years of post-generative AI experience. Historical analogies suggest that the full labor market effects of general-purpose technologies can take decades to materialize. The current evidence may therefore capture only the earliest phase of a much longer adjustment process — a concern reinforced by the productivity paradox literature, which documents repeated instances of technologies showing limited initial impact before producing transformative effects once complementary organizational and institutional changes ar"
=== H3 titles cellcog:
['1.1 AI and the Fourth Industrial Revolution', '1.2 Historical Context: Lessons from Prior Technological Transitions', '1.3 Scope, Methodology, and Analytical Framework', '2.1 The Task-Based Model: Displacement and Reinstatement', '2.2 Skill-Biased and Routine-Biased Technological Change', '2.3 Creative Destruction and Compensation Theory', '2.4 Directed Technical Change and the Role of Institutions', '2.5 Labor Process Theory and the Politics of AI Deployment', '3.1 Industrial Robots and Manufacturing Labor Markets', '3.2 Occupation-Level Exposure Studies', '3.3 Job Polarization and the Hollowing of Middle-Skill Work', '4.1 Task Exposure and the White-Collar Shift', '4.2 Productivity Effects and the Skill Compression Finding', '4.3 The Jagged Frontier: Limits of Augmentation', '4.4 From Exposure to Employment: Adoption Rates, Vacancy Declines, and the Entry-Level Bottleneck', '4.5 The Reinstatement Effect: New Tasks and New Roles', '5.1 Physical AI: Manufacturing and Logistics', '5.2 Analytical AI: Finance and Professional Services', '5.3 Generative AI: Creative Industries, Customer Service, and Education', '5.4 Clinical AI: Healthcare', '6.1 Occupation and Skill-Level Effects', '6.2 The Gender Dimension: Occupational Sorting and Unequal Access', '6.3 The Entry-Level Bottleneck and Intergenerational Effects', '6.4 Geographic and Global Dimensions: The North-South Divide', '6.5 Job Quality: Algorithmic Management, Deskilling, and Surveillance', '7.1 The Denmark-U.S. Divergence: Institutions as the Key Variable', '7.2 Reskilling, Education, and the Adult Learning Gap', '7.3 Toward Inclusive Transition: Social Dialogue, Regulation, and Worker Voice', '8.1 What the Evidence Converges On', '8.2 Outstanding Tensions and Unresolved Questions', '8.3 Limitations of the Current Literature', '8.4 Directions for Future Research']
=== H3 titles tavily:
['2.1 Task‑Based Models: Displacement, Productivity, and Reinstatement', '2.2 Skill‑Biased and Routine‑Biased Technological Change', '2.3 Superstar Firms, Winner‑Take‑All Dynamics, and Concentration', '2.4 Platformization and Algorithmic Management', '2.5 Organizational Complementarities and Sociotechnical Systems', '3.1 Automation of Tasks and Job Displacement', '3.2 Augmentation of Work and Productivity Gains', '3.3 Creation of New Occupations and Tasks', '3.4 Changes in Skill Requirements and the Acceleration of Skill Change', '3.5 Wage Polarization, Inequality, and Wage Dynamics', '3.6 Employment Reallocation Across Occupations, Industries and Regions', '4.1 Skill Level: High, Medium, and Low Skills', '4.2 Occupation Type: Routine vs Non‑Routine; Cognitive vs Manual', '4.3 Gender', '4.4 Age', '4.5 Geography and Region', '5.1 Manufacturing and Logistics', '5.2 Finance and Professional Services', '5.3 Healthcare', '5.4 Transportation, Delivery, and Platform‑Mediated Services', '5.5 Creative Industries and Media', '5.6 Public Administration, HR, and Recruitment', '6.1 Inequality and Social Cohesion', '6.2 Governance, Regulation, and Collective Voice', '6.3 Reskilling, Education, and Lifelong Learning', '7.1 Broad Consensus in the Literature', '7.2 Uncertainties and Debates', 'Sources']

stats('hf_cellcog_max.jsonl','drb_cellcog_max (cited)')

content.content:
### 4.3 Gender

Gendered impacts arise from both occupational segregation and algorithmic bias:

- Women are over‑represented in clerical and administrative roles that are **highly automatable**, exposing them to disproportionate automation risk.[1][12]
- Without targeted policies, AI‑driven restructuring can **reinforce gender segregation**, limiting women’s access to emerging high‑skill AI roles and exacerbating gender wage gaps.[12]
- At the same time, exposure to robots appears to reduce wages more for men, reflecting male concentration in manufacturing and robot‑intensive sectors.[13]

UNESCO and related work highlight gender biases embedded in AI systems and stress the importance of **gender‑sensitive AI governance**, especially in recruitment, performance evaluation and monitoring tools.[2][23]

### 4.4 Age

Evidence by age is less developed but conceptual and early empirical work

- Our synthesis is that these findings are not in tension; they document institutional mediation at work. Platforms lack internal labor markets, employment protection, notice periods

content.text: All checks done against the primary source (I re-downloaded the cited HF file and byte-verified it against the local corpus copy; md5 `c02f27b3…` matches `/home/polaris/polaris_project/drb_corpus/cellcog.jsonl`).

input.reasoning:
WHAT I DID: downloaded the cited file (https://huggingface.co/.../data/raw_data/drb_cellcog_max.jsonl, HTTP 200, 6,773,828 B, md5 c02f27b3217960104447e91388948609 — byte-identical to the local copy /home/polaris/polaris_project/drb_corpus/cellcog.jsonl), plus tavily-research.jsonl (md5 64ed2c35…, identical to local tavily.jsonl), the space file tree, both leaderboard.csv files, and per-model race_result.txt.

CONFIRMED FROM THE CITED FILE (task 72, "literature review on the restructuring impact of AI on the labor market"):
- drb_cellcog_max task 72: 32 H3, ZERO bullet lines, 168 non-heading prose paragraphs, mean 73.5 words/para. Claim said 32 H3 / 0 bullets / 168 paras / ~75 w — all correct.
- The section-8.2 quote is VERBATIM. "### 8.2 Outstanding Tensions and Unresolved Questions … The most fundamental is the gap between task-level productivity evidence and aggregate employment outcomes … This could mean that AI's labor market impact is still early and will intensify; that productivity gains are being absorbed through shorter hours, task expansion, or organizational slack rather than headcount reduction; or that general equilibrium effects — new demand, new products, new tasks — are already offsetting displacement. The current evidence cannot definitively distinguish among these explanations." Its rhetorical structure (contradiction between two literatures → three candidate reconciliations → what evidence cannot settle) is as described.
- Tavily task 72: 5,339 words, 28 H3, 141 bullets, 98 paras averaging 16.4 words. Its subsections literally use the bolded-lead-in-then-bullets pattern, including the exact string "Evidence from advanced economies:", with each bullet carrying its own [n] citation markers. "It LISTS" is accurate.
- H3 titles: Tavily's are bare topic labels ("4.3 Gender", "4.4 Age", "5.3 Healthcare"). cellcog's include the four cited claim-bearing ones ("…Skill Compression Finding", "The Jagged Frontier: Limits of Augmentation", "The Denmark-U.S. Divergence: Institutions as the Key Variable", "The Entry-Level Bottleneck") — though this is cherry-picked: most of cellcog's 32 H3s are also plain topic labels ("1.1 AI and the Fourth Industrial Revolution", "5.4 Clinical AI: Healthcare").

WHERE IT IS STALE / WRONG:
1. THE CITED FILE IS NOT THE #1 SYSTEM'S ARTIFACT. The space now has two leaderboards. data/ (legacy Gemini-2.5-Pro judge, 45 systems): drb_cellcog_max scores 56.67 = rank 7, behind qianfan_deepresearch_0430 (58.03); tavily-research is 52.44 = rank 23. data_gpt55/ (current GPT-5.5 judge, 10 systems): cellcog-max is #1 at 55.78 — but its submission there is a DIFFERENT file, data_gpt55/raw_data/cellcog-max.jsonl (11,492,296 B, md5 51b1d954fc377936e13b270b97c27d0d), not the 6.77 MB drb_cellcog_max.jsonl that was cited. So the claim reads the #7 artifact on the superseded judge and labels it "the #1 system." Tavily is not on the current leaderboard at all, so "#1 vs Tavily" is a cross-leaderboard comparison.
2. In the ACTUAL current-#1 file, task 72 is 16,334 words / 31 H3 / 215 paragraphs / 0 bullets — and it has NO "8.2 Outstanding Tensions and Unresolved Questions" section. The quoted paragraph does not exist in the #1 system's output.
3. Numbers don't reproduce. Cited file: 12,693 words (whitespace split) or 12,572 (\w+ regex) — not 12,937. Ratio to Tavily is 2.38x, not 2.15x (2.15x only appears if you mix tokenizers).
4. The marker statistics (2.40 vs 0.83 per 1k; corpus-wide 0.43/1k) are not reproducible — no lexicon is given, and no plausible contrast/inference lexicon I tried lands near those values (a standard one gives 12.1 vs 10.3 per 1k on task 72). Also "11 systems" cannot be checked; the space exposes 30 raw_data corpora on the legacy board.

WHAT SURVIVES: the underlying mechanism claim is directionally right and, importantly, still holds on the real #1 artifact. The current #1 cellcog-max task-72 report also has zero bullets and ~70-word prose paragraphs, and it does cross-source reconciliation explicitly: "Two well-identified findings sit in apparent tension… Our synthesis is that these findings are not in tension; they document institutional mediation at work." My own corpus-wide proxy (contrast/inference lexicon, English reports only, 10 systems) ranks tavily LOWEST (2.86/1k) and cellcog-max near the top (5.21/1k), which matches the claimed ordering even though the absolute figures do not.

Verdict STALE rather than FALSE: the evidence quoted is real and correctly transcribed, and the qualitative thesis replicates, but it is drawn from a superseded submission scored by the retired Gemini-2.5-Pro judge, where the system was #7, and several of the exact statistics are wrong.
input.corrected:
Correct framing: on the CURRENT DeepResearch-Bench leaderboard (GPT-5.5 RACE judge, data_gpt55/leaderboard.csv), cellcog-max is #1 with 55.78 — but its scored submission is data_gpt55/raw_data/cellcog-max.jsonl (11.5 MB, md5 51b1d954fc377936e13b270b97c27d0d), NOT the cited data/raw_data/drb_cellcog_max.jsonl (6.77 MB, md5 c02f27b3217960104447e91388948609). The cited file belongs to the legacy Gemini-2.5-Pro-judged board, where it ranks #7/45 (56.67, behind qianfan_deepresearch_0430 at 58.03) and tavily-research ranks #23 (52.44); tavily is absent from the current board entirely.

Task 72 in the cited (non-#1) file: 12,693 words (2.38x Tavily's 5,339), 32 H3, 0 bullets, 168 prose paragraphs @ 73.5 words — the "12,937 words / 2.15x" figures are wrong. The quoted 8.2 paragraph is verbatim correct in that file.
